original_abstract: The global water crisis—encompassing pollution, overextraction, ecosystem collapse, and inequitable access—persists despite decades of infrastructure investment and efficiency improvements. Progress toward Sustainable Development Goal 6 remains insufficient, largely due to policy inertia, regulatory capture, and a narrow focus on economic growth. This paper proposes a "Beyond Growth" framing to rethink modern water responses. Grounded in systems thinking, justice principles, and relationality, this perspective prioritizes the needs of the most marginalized and advocates for the decoupling of economic development from environmental harm. By shifting from traditional "build and grow" models toward justice-principled institutional innovations and inclusive governance, a Beyond Growth approach offers a transformative pathway toward a sustainable and equitable water future.
humanized_abstract: Despite significant investment in infrastructure and improvements in efficiency, the global water crisis—defined by the degradation of water quality, the depletion of aquifers, the destruction of wetlands, and the inability of people to access sufficient, clean water—continues unabated. The achievement of SDG6 is being threatened by lock-in effects stemming from the legacy of previous decisions, the increasing dominance of economic interests over public authorities and the continuing priority given to development in the water sector. An alternative perspective called the “Beyond Growth” approach is outlined in this working paper. Based on systems thinking, justice, and relationality, the Beyond Growth approach prioritises the needs of the most vulnerable and removes environmental, water-related or land-related barriers to the development of all others. By moving away from conventional build-and-grow models in favour of justice-principled institutional innovations and participatory governance, the Beyond Growth approach opens up a new perspective on how a just and sustainable water future can be achieved.
